name: ING
mission: The entire experience of planning a series of seminars with ING was inspirational. They did an amazing job. The ING team welcomed input along the way which allowed each session to be even better than the one before it. We will continue to work with ING as I am confident that they will continue to provide value in our work.

zakat.claim_evidence: The search results for 'The Islamic Networks Group (ING)' describe it as a 'left-progressive advocacy group promoting the interests of Muslims living in the United States'. There is no explicit mention on their website or in the provided search snippets of accepting zakat donations, having dedicated zakat funds or campaigns, or serving the 8 asnaf categories. The information focuses on their advocacy, educational programs, and funding from foundations. (Source: https://www.influencewatch.org/non-profit/the-islamic-networks-group/)

ideal_donor_profile.best_for_summary: Best for donors who value long-term interfaith dialogue and educational outreach within the US and are comfortable supporting an established institution with higher administrative costs.

giving_considerations: High overhead costs mean a smaller portion of gifts reaches direct programs

ideal_donor_profile.not_ideal_for: Donors seeking high-efficiency direct humanitarian aid or those looking to fulfill zakat obligations.

impact_evidence.evidence_grade_explanation: Grade C reflects that while the organization tracks reach and satisfaction, it lacks independent, third-party impact evaluations.
impact_evidence.theory_of_change_summary: ING creates impact by training speakers and educators to provide accurate information about Islam, thereby reducing prejudice and fostering inclusion.
impact_evidence.why_evidence_matters: Evidence ensures that educational programs actually change perceptions and reduce bias in the long term.

long_term_outlook.room_for_funding_explanation: With a <cite id="41">revenue CAGR of 6.0%</cite> and a small total budget, the organization has significant room to absorb more funding for program expansion.

strengths.detail: operating since 1993, the organization has delivered <cite id="10">780 interfaith panels</cite> and trained <cite id="11">200 Muslim teens</cite> in a single year.
The organization maintains significant online visibility with <cite id="12">50,000 monthly website visitors</cite> and a <cite id="13">96% educator satisfaction rating</cite>.
The charity holds <cite id="14">5.2 months of working capital</cite> in working capital, providing high organizational resilience.

strengths_deep_dive: Islamic Networks Inc excels in creating high-quality educational content that resonates with professional audiences. Their 2020 data showed <cite id="15">400,000 live audience attendees</cite> and an <cite id="16">educator effectiveness rating of 89%</cite>. This impact is amplified by digital assets, with <cite id="17">870,000 views of recorded talks</cite> reported in the same period. Their ability to bridge cultural gaps is further evidenced by their grantmaking, where they distributed <cite id="18">$72,117 in domestic grants</cite> to partners like Muslim Americans in Public Service.
rich_narrative.summary: founded in 1993, Islamic Networks Inc (ING) is a non-profit organization dedicated to <cite id="1">fostering a deeper, more nuanced understanding of Muslims</cite> and other faith-based, racial, and ethnic communities. The organization operates through a model of <cite id="2">educational outreach and interfaith engagement</cite>, utilizing programs like the Islamic Speakers Bureau and specialized Inclusion and Belonging Training. Despite its long history, the organization maintains a relatively small financial footprint, reporting <cite id="3">total revenue of $622,869</cite> in 2023. Its impact is primarily driven through <cite id="4">capacity building and professional development</cite> for educators and community leaders. While the organization has achieved a <cite id="5">projected annual reach of 2 million people</cite> through its digital and physical resources, it faces significant financial efficiency challenges. Currently, the organization allocates a <cite id="6">program expense ratio of 39.5%</cite>, which is substantially below the <cite id="7">peer program ratio median of 85.3%</cite> for similar religious and cultural organizations. This suggests that a majority of its annual budget is directed toward administrative and fundraising functions rather than direct service delivery. However, the organization demonstrates <cite id="8">high fundraising efficiency</cite>, reporting a cost of $0.00 per $1 raised, and maintains a resilient financial cushion with <cite id="9">5.2 months of working capital</cite> in reserves.
